name: keplergl (Large-Scale Geospatial Visualization)

auth.notes: Mapbox token required for basemap tiles; data visualization itself is local

performance.notes: WebGL rendering via deck.gl; handles millions of points in browser

agent_readiness.notes: Jupyter-based large-scale geospatial visualization. from keplergl import KeplerGl. map_1 = KeplerGl(height=600). map_1.add_data(data=df, name='trip data'). map_1 (in Jupyter). map_1.save_to_html(file_name='map.html'). Config: map_1 = KeplerGl(height=600, config={...}). map_1.config to get current config. Accepts: pandas DataFrame, geopandas GeoDataFrame, dict of records, CSV string. Layer types (via UI): point, arc, line, hexbin, heatmap, GeoJSON polygon, H3 hexagon, trip animation. Requires Mapbox token for basemap. KeplerGl(data={'layer1': df1, 'layer2': df2}). Works in Jupyter Lab and Notebook. Export to HTML for sharing.

gotchas: Requires Mapbox token for base map tiles — create free account at mapbox.com
Jupyter-only visualization — HTML export for non-Jupyter sharing
Config serialization for reproducibility is complex — UI-generated JSON
No type annotations — limited IDE support
Limited programmatic layer control — most config done via UI or JSON config blob
best_when: Large-scale geospatial data exploration in Jupyter (millions of points)
Point, arc, H3, heatmap, and trip animation layers
Quick interactive geospatial EDA for data science workflows
avoid_when: Non-Jupyter environments (use pydeck or deck.gl directly)
When programmatic layer control is needed (use pydeck)
Static map generation (use folium or matplotlib with cartopy)
summary: keplergl brings Kepler.gl interactive geospatial visualization to Jupyter. KeplerGl().add_data(df). Millions of points via WebGL/deck.gl. Point, arc, H3, heatmap, trip layers. Export to HTML. MIT by Uber/Foursquare. Mapbox token required for basemap. Jupyter-only. Config via UI or JSON blob. Best for large-scale geospatial EDA. Use pydeck for programmatic layer control.
